Well, let's get started. So, you know, the first rule to a six-figure a day sales letter is that it must have what I call a James Bond worthy opening. So, if you see the James Bond movies, and I got this analogy from one of my other grating mentors, Evan Pagan, James Bond movies, they don't start off slow. So, you know, if you remember the movie GoldenEye, the way it starts out is this guy, some dude dressed in all black, and he's like running around like, you know, like, who knows what he's doing, like back in like some like crazy, like mountainous cavern. He runs up to the edge of a dam that's like, you know, dozens of stories high. He takes a bungee cord, he touches the top of the dam, bungee jumps off the top of the dam, and then at the end of his bungee jump, he shoots another gun that puts a cord into the ground that allows him to repel and come down the dam. And then he starts like planting a bomb or some secret stuff, and you don't know what, and you're just like, what the hell is this guy doing? I gotta know exactly where this goes, you know, it's very, very gripping. So, that's something that I have found is common through the threads of Six Figure Day sales letters. Oh, and by the way, in this presentation, I'm gonna talk about some Six Figure Day letters that I've written. I've written five or six of them now, depending on, you know, how you tabulate it. And then we're gonna tell it about some that other people have written. But I was just going through some of these examples, and one of them that comes to mind is a letter called The End of America, and this was written by a guy named Mike Palmer, who is with Stansbury Research and Agora Publishing, and this thing had a James Bond-worthy opening because it started off talking about, and this was launched in around 2009 when the recession was going on, and it started talking about this recession and how people didn't know how bad it could get. This could turn into, I remember there was a phrase in there that said, you need to riot in the streets, alright, and it just painted that picture, like thinking of that in the United States, and people were like, whoa, where is this going? And then this thing, it was like some people are gonna get rich in the ensuing financial crisis, and you could be one of them. And I thought that was pretty James Bond-worthy, and I actually pulled out a book written by some of the guys that were going to go to publishing when I was traveling through the seminar, written by Mark Ford, I'm sorry, John Ford, and Mark Ford, who goes by Michael Masterson, and there was a headline in there. I don't know if this is a six-figure or a day letter, but I love this headline. It says, Texas Company tapping 2.8 trillion oil reserve under the Eiffel Tower. And you read that and you're just like, what the hell is going on here? You know, something, you just had to figure out, like, how is this happening, and what's going on? Is James Bond-worthy for sure? I found that the secret to a James Bond-worthy opening is, first of all, you don't want to tell them exactly what you are talking about. You want to use what's called a secret lead, and that means that, like, let's say you're trying to sell a health or fitness product, like, your opening line is not going to be like, hey, here's how you get in shape, or if you're trying to sell financial newsletter, you're not going to say something like, hey, here's how to pick winning stocks. You know, you're going to talk about something big that's a crazy event, in some cases, a catastrophic event and create that curiosity. But there's got to be a benefit in there that lets them know that what they're going to watch is going to let them know some valuable information. So I'd like to pull open a multiple six-figure-a-day sales letter that I did write myself and give you an example of what I consider to be a James Bond-worthy opening for something that, you know, I don't know, I mean, James Bond would probably use this product, but I don't think it would be featured in the movie. You know, it's something pretty everyday that average Americans, I think every average American should be using this as a product that I use every day, but let's pull it up, alright? Okay, so people can go back and watch it on their own. But it goes next. It says, governments are finally admitting that this is real. Measures have been proposed in New York and the country of Mexico to try and stop it. If I try to read this, it may already be too late. My name is Craig Guepetta. I'm the director of science and nutrition of whole body research international. And I spent the last 24 months working with my team of doctors and scientists to figure out a way to stop this parasite. Perhaps the scariest thing is that its symptoms come on slowly. You may never even realize your fatigue, weight gain, or lack of sleep is the result of this bad bug until it finally takes over and forces you to seek medical attention. Okay, so that's the hook, so to speak, about this parasite. And then in this presentation, I'm going to kind of shorten the gist of the presentation right now for you guys so we can talk about the whole thing. The way that I came across this is because of sugar. So a lot of people don't know the real story of sugar, but sugar has been around since the 17th and 1800s. And there have been studies done even back then in the 1800s by scientists that show sugar is terrible for human and animal consumption. However, the sugar people who were producing it knew it was a very high profitable crop. So they've actually been offering money to farmers even as far as the 1800s to show that if they can do studies to show that it was healthy. So in this letter, I talk about some of the studies that were done, and I'm going to pull it back up here and just like scroll down to one. So it talks about how in 1908, the Committee of West India, a large sugar conglomerate of the time, appeared before the British House of Commons to offer a prize of 25 guineas, about $1,000 in today's coin. Anyone who could show an experiment that proved sugar was good for feeding and fattening cows, hogs, and sheep. Food for animals, as all was expensive. Sugar was extremely cheap, so many farmers took on the challenge hoping for good results. The 25 guineas would be just a bonus to the new cheaper food supply. But as you may have guessed, the attempts were a disaster. Many resulted in the death of livestock. One member of parliament, John Kerwin, took on the challenge himself trying to feed sugar and lasses to his calves after the experiment of this well-known, highly ranked politician failed, but many of West India gave up. This sales letter has got many stories in here of how the sugar conglomerates have been trying to convince people that sugar is healthy, dating as far back as the 1800s. And I give some modern ones of stuff they were doing in the 60s, 70s, and 80s. And some of them are quite frankly, unfucking believable. So if you're familiar with Aspartame, which is more commonly known as Nutrisweep, when Aspartame came out, the company GD-SURL, who produced it, wanted to get it into the marketplace because they knew it would be a billion dollar brand if they could produce it. However, there was one little problem. And that was that Aspartame and all the lab tests on animals caused tumors to grow in the animals. So what GD-SURL did is they had the scientists cut the tumors out of the animals and sew them back up and write the tests up as though those tumors were never there. They then tried to submit Aspartame to the FDA for approval. The FDA found out about their forged test results. And for the first time ever, the United States government pressed criminal charges against a commodity manufacturing company for falsifying studies. So that happened. They went back to the drawing boards and eventually they were able to get Aspartame approved. And I'm going to share that story with you in just a minute along with this presentation. But yeah, that happened. It sounds kind of freaking crazy that that could happen in North America, but it did. And that's the type of story that you can't make this shit up story. And the next one I'm going to tell you guys in a little bit about how Aspartame did finally get approved is even wilder. But people are hearing this stuff and they can't take their eyes off because they just can't believe it's happening. And as you'll note, when I go back to this page here, so the next story I'm going to share with you is how the NutriStreet got published. Here it is. It's talking about artificial sweeteners. I mean, this is like 20 minutes into the video or something like that. And you'll notice that they don't have any idea that there's anything for sale yet. So that's why Michael Masterson and John Ford called this the secret lead because it's like that. They don't realize that they think they're just getting information. They are. They're just getting valuable information. It hasn't transitioned to any type of sales presentation yet. And if you want to capture big audiences, like Gary Bencevanga says, you've got to provide value up front. You've got to give away good free content. It's not just all about the sale. So I tend to go up on a lot of tangents when I'm talking. So I'll tell you guys another little story right now that I was talking to a guy just the other day and telling him how to scale big on the Internet with his product. And this guy's got a product that shows people how to play the lottery from their cell phone. You can buy lottery tickets on your phone. And he's telling me, he's like, yeah, so I want to get everyone who plays the lottery to go on their cell phone and play it with me instead of going to the bodega and buying the tickets. I said, well, let's find a Danny and you can build a damn good business off of that. But why not scale it bigger and try to get everyone who's over 18 years old? Because that's your real market is because anyone is over 18 is allowed to play the lottery, right? You don't need to go after existing people. And the same is true with this product. So what this sales letter eventually does sell is a supplement and you could do it. You know, advertising campaign on like Google AdWords targeted for people who are typing in like, you know, best supplement for weight loss or best supplement for digestion or anything like that. And you know, there's a small amount of people that are typing that stuff in and looking for supplements. You know, you can, a strong copywriter can grab those people with a message about supplements and which one's the best for X, Y and Z and good proof run is claims. However, if you want to create a promotion that rocks the entire Internet, so to speak, you got to be able to get the attention of someone who's just browsing around on the Huffington Post, TMZ, Forbes, you know, reading about their recipes or sports or, you know, finance or whatever they are interested in that has nothing to do with whatever you want to talk about. So I figured out how can you hook that person and get them on board and take them down that direction. And that's the, you know, overlying secret to all of this and how to write a six-figure day letter, which we're going to get into the next thing. It must have killed the majority of Americans. And this is true that your product needs to be like applicable, like that lottery product is good for anyone over 18. And this is the supplement that you're going to find out about in this presentation. I feel every American should be taking it of any age. But yeah, it's got to kill the majority and your campaign has to be able to capture the attention of the majority also and shooting back the way you can make your campaign. It's the majority of the Americans as you start with a James Bond worthy opening and then you give them a, you can't make this shit up story. Yeah, I just got a little confused on something. So I'm late, but I was like so bummed when Bond told me I was like, wait a minute, the ZSD or PT? I told you, I even told them that you'd be pissed off knowing that you were missing. Yeah, I was. I wanted to ask you a quick question, Craig. When you're creating the story, now I don't believe in absolutes all the time because there are certain situations that's not right. But the one thing that's a common theme that I see in the James Bond openings, and by the way, who is this James Bond character anyway? But I like the openings that attract their attention and the headlines you've shown so far are something that I talk a lot about with people that I'm wondering how you feel about it. When it comes to the stories and stuff, if everything's considered equal, or everything else is equal and you have two chances, do you think that the dark side stories pull more attention and more rabid in? Because there's the type of the story that says, hey, this guy's just created a new thing that will eliminate these cravings and all these bad stuff and this parasite that's out there. Or there's the dark side, hey, there's this big do-me-parasite out there. And what I find, I think, is when given the chance that a lot of the times human nature, to grab that attention, wants the dark side. And I'm just wondering what your thoughts on that, because there's a way to go positive and a way to go negative with anything that you want to say. And I just noticed this underlying theme about a confession seems to be more powerful than somebody who's actually just helping somebody, as far as grabbing the attention. This is just the attention part. And I'm wondering about that, because I noticed a lot of the stuff you shared has that dark side component to it. Yeah, you know, that's a great question. These things do have the dark side component, but I actually do. I don't think it's necessary. I think this type of opening would work just as well. And I have another promotion running right now that's very successful, that has a positive opening. And it's in the anti-aging space. And I don't want to give away all my secrets today, so I don't want to tell exactly what this one says, but it tells a true story of a super athlete who is in their shoes. I think it's a story of the 70s. And it's a really upbeat story, and it just gives people a lot of hope that, like, hey, just because you're getting older doesn't mean your time to shine is through. So I think it can open with something more positive and be just successful. I have seen that work also. However, I will say that you need a little bit of both in any sales letter in order to keep someone intrigued. You've got to create that roller coaster of emotions. So while we're talking about that 70-year-old super athlete, you have to let them know whether it's on their mind or not that there are people who are 70 who are starting to see the first signs of dementia, are not able to place forts at the levels that they used to. They're not able to play around a tennis without feeling aches and pains afterwards. You have to talk to that as well. So I recommend thinking about what I've always said is sell the dream, but you have to explain the nightmare also to make them realize how great that dream is going to be. And you need to do that in different parts of the letter too, all throughout. So in the opening, yeah, it's important. This one that I'm sharing right now is pretty negative, but it's also very damn compelling. However, I've fixed a lot of letters that come through from my team because I have my own team of copywriters now that have come up and been too negative. I'm like, no, no, no, people are going to read this and they're going to get depressed. So you have to have indications of the dream in there usually. This one might be an exception to that rule because we're not promising benefits to them just yet, but I have written other sales letters to actually sell similar products in this same supplement space. I've said, no, this is too negative and let's add some things in the beginning of the letter that say, hey, this is, here's what's wrong now. You might be feeling a little tired, things like that. However, it doesn't have to be this way. Today, we're going to show you how to get your energy back and improve the way you feel and sharpen your memory and all that stuff, gives you some positivity at the beginning as well. Yeah, great. I just always wonder about that attention thing and I don't believe in absolutes myself. Yeah, and let me go off on another tangent here since I love love tangents. I was just at a seminar where Dan Kennedy spoke and he had the presentation. He was my favorite presentation I've ever seen of Dan Kennedy. He was talking about selling alarm systems and how back in the day they would go door to door selling alarm systems and they would have marginal results because it was prevention and people weren't thinking of a bad thing that could happen. He's talking about a lot of alarm companies nowadays will make the same mistake with the ad campaign as they'll show grandma sleeping at home soundly and be like, yeah, I get your alarm system so grandma can sleep at home and not to worry. But then he makes a point that what these old salesman used to do, dirty salesman literally would, before they'd come to a neighborhood, they'd go in the bushes and they'd throw in in the bushes empty whiskey bottles, condom wrappers and porno magazines. And they do that for a week or two before they go around selling alarm systems and of course when they did come around knocking door to door they'd sell a hell of a lot more systems. So he said, you know, you can sell a couple of alarm systems showing grandma sleeping soundly but you'll sell a lot more with the whiskey bottles, condom wrappers and the porno. Sugarman did that with the Midas alarm. What he figured out was he went into the neighborhoods that are already having a robbery incident or something like that and that was the way that they were softened up. Also the answer, can you guys hear me? You guys can hear me, right? Okay, the other thing I was going to say is that, you know, tomorrow we're going to do something on my dad's thing and one of the things he says in his ad that I was going to point out, my mind's going to point it now, is that when he gets into a sad story, what he did is he does a break and he wrote in the desperate housewife ad. Don't get me wrong, I don't want this to sound like a sad story. I'm not telling you all this because I want you to feel sorry for me. There's no need for that anyway. My story has a very happy ending. So right in the first like four turns, I don't know if you use it because I was late on the thing but I always remember that term, you always use turn of the pain and he's definitely turning up the pain in the first four paragraphs of that ad. But he knew he might be pushing that threshold so he knew to take a time out and not get the prospects so just pressed that they just checked out of the pitch. Yeah, yeah, nice. All right, so let's jump into rule number four. Rule number four for a six figure day sales letter is you must write a current trend. So some mistakes I see people make are trying to sell something that has already phased in and phased out or is something that Americans aren't thinking about. And is like too away from their mind that you can consider as an option or also something that's too saturated that too many people are doing. So this letter that I just revealed here now with the American parasite theme is selling a probiotic supplement. And what probiotics are most now most people know what they are. It's beneficial bacteria that you take to balance out the bad bacteria that can grow inside of your GI tract if you're eating foods with too much sugar than artificial sweetener. So now these days people know what probiotics are. So when we first lost this letter my brother who does our market research for our companies was looking into probiotics and he looked on Google Trends which is a Google track service search terms and how often things are appearing in blogs and articles and things like that. And he saw this trend for probiotics ticking up. It's just like starting to gain traction in the last few years. And we caught this thing as it was like had a little bit of traction people are just starting to get onto it. And you can actually see if you type in Google Trends on probiotics you can see this graph going up and then you can see the day our video went live and it goes bam, shoots way up. So we actually raised the entire market for probiotics. We were the number one seller of probiotics in the world in the first quarter of 2014 in part of this campaign and there were so many knockoffs and things like that. That's actually one of the reasons I finally decided to share it today because this thing is already been knocked off so many times. But now the probiotic market has saturated the shit. I don't think you can do the same thing with a probiotic right now because now the timing is wrong. Now people know they can go to Whole Foods, GNC since then has come up with their own line of probiotics that are a lot less expensive than the one we were selling here. So you can't have the same margins and this and that. But yeah we capitalized on that. I see these things like fade in and fade out. The person who creates a lot of trends in this diet and supplements space is Dr. Oz. Dr. Oz had been pushing that green coffee bean as a weight loss thing for the last three years. And recently it got debunked as Dr. Oz wanting ratings or something like that. The doctor he brought in his show was actually a fake doctor. That guy is just like, I don't know, I won't go down that tangent about Dr. Oz. But I will say that I see a lot of people making that mistake and they'll be like, hey I'm going to go start selling green coffee bean right now. And they won't know that green coffee bean was hot back in the day but it never really had a lot of clinical studies. But there was a time when if someone had started selling green coffee bean they could have caught that up spike. But now would be a terrible time to do it. And that's why actually I feel that while there were some knockoffs in this video, we were running this thing so big and buying so much crap at the time the knockoffs came up. It was actually kind of on its way down. And actually we run campaigns smaller than that these days because we want to keep it alive because this thing did well. It had a big rise. It had a pretty fast burnout for other reasons than that. So yeah, number four, must write current trends. Let's do one and see what my next rule is. Okay, so the next rule is must have massive internal credibility. And what this means is that if you're going to sell on a grand scale you've got to have something that is a good product. And that's why green coffee bean will never be as big as probiotics were because green coffee bean is quote unquote bullshit and probiotics really are. Most doctors believe more important to take every day that a daily multivitamin. And if you do a search for doctors, just the phrase doctors, probiotics, you'll see it's just a movement right now. It's been going on in Europe and Asia for many, many years, but it hadn't really hit America yet. So we put all those doctor quotes right into this letter and I'll pull it back up again and we can scroll down to some of them. So here is Dr. David Bromont on the Dr. Raj show and we've got a quote, and he had the current New York Times bestseller when this letter came out. His book was the number one bestseller in the New York Times and on Amazon and everything. And he said talking, oh, and this is what he said about the American parasite, which is actually more commonly known as Candida. He said, those of us who deal with this health condition in a clinical setting are witnessing what now almost seems epidemic. So that was a doctor quote to prove that this thing I'm talking about is affecting all these people is real. I mean, David Bromont, it's almost epidemic. And then I also put in here, I'm going to do a little search because that Rice University study. I'm going to move here. There it is in 2005. Okay, I did remember slightly often in 2005, molecular biologists at Rice University estimated it was already affecting 70% of the American population. That was back in 2005, you know, so it's pretty likely that it grew since then. So you need to have these massive, you need to have this massive internal credibility all throughout for everything you're talking about. So, you know, most writers and marketers realize that they have to have internal credibility when it comes to their product. So like, let's say a probiotics themselves, they have to have massive internal credibility from doctors saying a probiotics or something you should be taking every day. And they have to have clinical studies that show, you know, these people took, well, one clinical study shows that breastfeeding moms to probiotics and others took a placebo and the kids whose moms took the probiotic supplement, their children actually cried less. That's a real study that was done, you know, it's fascinating. And then there was another study done in the daycare center where they give kids probiotics and the kids who took the probiotics didn't call in as sick as nearly as much as the kids who didn't. So like, there's all these, you know, substantiating stuff that most writers will know that you've got to put that stuff in for the product. But if you're telling a memory what we talked about, you can't make this shit up story, you've got to have this type of internal credibility, massive internal credibility showing them that your story is real too. So that's why we have quotes in here from doctors just about the story, you know, you saw the Pearl Mother quote talking about how it was becoming an epidemic as people were coming into his clinic talking about how all those people were suffering from this. You saw the quote from the Rice University about us, they estimated 70% of Americans were affecting this. And then another internal credibility thing that we mentioned, if you remember at the beginning, it talks about, you know, I don't want to scroll through this too much because I might not find it. But it talks about how governments are finally admitting this is real. Measures have been proposed in New York and the country of Mexico to try and stop it. So that's in the very first opening thing of this letter. And then later we substantiated that by saying to people where that stuff came from and what it is, is that in New York City, Mayor Michael Bloomberg a couple years ago tried to ban large sodas. And the reason he does it is because they're high sugar content and people are getting too much sugar. And that's what he was trying to do, stop the bad effects of sugar on the human body because sugar is fucking bad shit. And if you read this whole letter of which every piece is trued substantiated with both in the letter and this stuff really happened, you'll see just how bad sugar is. And then the other thing was that the country of Mexico is now, for the first time ever, they're the first country to put high taxes on sugar just like America did on cigarettes and tobacco. They're putting the same type of taxes on refined sugars. So we talked about that in here. So you've got to have massive credibility for every part of your promotion or people aren't going to believe it. The consumer is much, much smarter, more educated these days and if they aren't, they can get that education right away. And we're going to talk about that when it comes to rule number seven. But right now let's go to rule number six. Okay, so actually again I hit on rule number six a little bit there. So if you want to have a six-figure-a-day sales letter, you still have to have the best product. You can't expect to scale huge unless your product is awesome. And the reason why is because you have to have this internal credibility for the product. So you need to talk about like Quad Hopkins did in the famous Schlitz beer campaign, the process of creating it and why you're doing it. Why yours is better than the rest. And I'm just going to jump ahead to rule number seven because these things kind of mesh together. The internal credibility, the rule number six must have the best product with proof. And then number seven is you must pass the tab test. So the tab test is something that a lot of internet marketers don't think about because this tab test was not around really so much in 2010. But what I'm talking about now is tabbed browsers, how everyone is using them. And these days when someone's watching one of your videos or reading one of your promotions, they can be like, I'm going to check this product out. Who is this Porter Stansbury that's talking about this end of America? Who is this guy that can pop it up, put his name in and see, you know, and it's like, you've got a lot of stuff in there saying, hey, you know, this guy's full of shit or whatever, or they pop in the name of your product. You know, what is this key biotics? Is this stuff real? And then there's like a review page up on Amazon and it's got like a one out of four stars. No one's going to buy it, you know, so your product has to pass the tab test. And that's why we take special care, you know, when we're setting out to write any sales letter and everyone should in instantiating why their product is the best choice in the market. Because these days, especially when you're selling something that's a commodity like a probiotic where they can go get it at GNC or Whole Foods or something like that. You know, it's a little bit less important with an information product, but still you got to show why yours is the best product out there and give them proof. So for those who aren't familiar with the Claude Hopkins Schlitz beer story, I've got one of the best ways to do that is to tell the story of how it's made and manufactured. Now, in the supplement business, this is very key because one thing I learned since I got into the supplement business is that a lot of supplements are dog shit, part of my French. And actually they just did an analysis of some of the top supplements at Walmart that someone went in and bought them, an independent research group working hand in hand with the FDA, bought the supplements, took them to third party labs for testing and found that they didn't meet the claims that were listed in the bottle. So one of the things we did in this letter is we not only explained our manufacturing process that talked about how we didn't allow any fillers, any toxins, but we also had them third party tested by an independent FDA approved laboratory. And that's something that people can really respond to. And we do that for our own safety as well, but it also protects the consumer. So if you're selling a product with a fantastic sales message and it's not a great product and there's ways for people to find out what should be a good product and it's not an issue like for probiotics, they can pop up and it's having to do that. So let's say they get to the end of this letter and it's like, hey, you know, what we recommend for fighting Candida is to take probiotics, there's beneficial good bacteria, they can go, oh, what are probiotics? Tab open. What to look for to probiotic? Hmm, it should have like X amount of colony forming units inside of it. It should have X amount of bacteria strains. There will be doctors that are telling them this, you know, and then they'll go back to your page and they'll be like, well, you know, Dr. David Perlmutter says that it should have 10 billion colony forming units in it. How much did this one have? Oh, it has 37 billion. Wow, this really is good. Okay, but Dr. David Perlmutter also says that it should have 10 strains of beneficial bacteria. Let's go back to this product. Oh, this is 14 strains. Okay, this is good stuff. Wait, does Dr. David Perlmutter recommend one himself? Oh, yes, he does, and it sells for 49.95. How much is this one? Oh, it's 39.95 and it's got 80 exceeds his recommendation. Okay, I mean, and back in the day when people were just doing the single long form letter, there wasn't browser tabs. People didn't know to open up multiple browsers. They didn't shop as much and you could get away with having those, what I call leaks in your letter or in your product. But these days you can't, and I'll tell you on a business standpoint too, that if you're selling junk, it's, well, I'll say, it's hard enough when you hit one of these winners, it's hard enough dealing with your customer service selling a great product. I can't even imagine what would happen if you're selling something where they get it and like, you know, let's say they have a third party test and it doesn't check out, or they go down to GNC and they see a much better deal or something like that. Like, I can't even imagine what kind of nightmare that would be. And then that destroys your tab test because they go review it and write up the doctor reviews about all your product and this and that. So, you know, if you're selling something on a small scale, you know, maybe you can get away with selling something that's not quite as good, but one of the big keys to doing this, doing massive volume on a long term is having a great product that people will spontaneously write great reviews about. And that's that tab test, you know. So, if you look up key biotics reviews, you'll see people love this product, it's got great reviews and all that. But I also told you the guys at this campaign was short lived, unfortunately, and that actually goes a little bit back to Bond's question earlier. And if you remember, at the beginning of this presentation, I talked about how, I was going to show you how to write a letter that breaks the entire internet. Well, this letter actually was, we were told by Google that they would not read it anymore. And the reason why, actually, I mean, they never tell you the exact reason, but they soon after, you know, maybe soon before they told us that, Google introduced two changes to their terms of service that affect all marketers. And one of them is that you cannot make people feel like they are a victim of a conspiracy. And I'll tell you what, like, it's kind of a bummer. So, now this is a, I think there's a marketing lesson in this somewhere, but I'm going to talk about, I'm going to take you guys through another section of this letter that puts it off the fucking charts when it comes to the can't make this shit up story. And it's absolutely true. And this is about artificial sweeteners. And this is it, this letter. And I think this is the reason Google didn't like it. But I'm going to read this, just this one section of sales letter to you now. And every word of it is true. And you can look it up. And let's see, where is it? Okay. Artificial sweeteners. Perhaps the most notorious of artificial sweeteners. Oh, you know, okay, I'm sorry, I forgot, I already told you guys about how it was in the animal studies causing tumors. But here's the next part of that. So I told you I was going to get back to the part about how it did get passed through, right? So you guys ready for that? Okay. So this happened, the government pressed criminal charges against GD Searle, who put this artificial sweetener into the marketplace. And then, you know, they've been investigation revealed that all these tumors were real and being cut out of animals. So I'm going to start from here. What would follow was a set of dirty political tricks that must have made Abraham Lincoln roll over in his grave. GD Searle first arranged to actually hire the US Attorney who was leading the investigation. Samuel Skinner first resigned from the US Attorney's office stalling the investigation, then went to work for Searle. Later that year, Donald Rumsfeld was hired on as a CEO of Searle. Yes, the same Donald Rumsfeld that was Secretary of Defense during the Bush administration. He appointed several political buddies to top management positions at Searle and proclaimed to you get aspartame approved within one year. At the time, Reagan had just been elected and Rumsfeld was part of Reagan's transition team who was in charge of choosing a new FDA commissioner. They appointed a man named Arthur Howe Hayes Jr. One of Arthur Hayes' first acts as FDA commissioner was to appoint a five-person panel to review the final decision to reject aspartame or nutrition. Three of the five panel members still voted not to approve it, citing the animal tumors as a big concern. So what did Hayes do? Simple. He appointed a sixth member to tie the vote at 3-3. Then Hayes, a man who knew nothing of food additives, decided he as a commissioner should get a vote also. He then cast the deciding vote himself in favor of approval. Shortly after this scheme, Hayes resigned from his position as FDA commissioner after which he was hired by Searle for a position paying hundreds of thousands of dollars a year. I know this sounds like a tale from a political horror movie, but sadly it is 100% true. After all was said and done, aspartame was approved, Coca-Cola began using it to make Diet Coke, which they then marketed as a weight loss drink. In that first year alone, the FDA received over 600 consumer complaints, reporting headaches, gizziness, and other strange reactions to this new foreign substance our bodies were never designed to handle. But the food conglomerates were the one, and since then, over the last 30 years, more and more of these refined sugars, artificial sweeteners, preservatives, and other unnatural elements have infiltrated our food supply, creating essentially a new diet without ever giving us a choice. Alright, so how freaking crazy is that? Jesus. Okay, so I was having a talk with John Benson, you know, he's a part of this program, right? So I didn't, I wouldn't wear this, there was actually one part of this letter, I had the timing a little bit wrong, but aspartame was actually approved the day Reagan was voted into office. For a while was that. Wow. So, you know, the story reminds me of Chaney voting himself to be vice president for Bush's team, because they hired Chaney to actually feel out and decide who should be his vice president. He took the money and then told them, me. Hey, I mean, you know, it happens every day, and I brought it to the attention of the American public with this story that, you know, they can't deny it's true, and you know, they, Google felt like it was making people feel like they were a victim of conspiracy, and now that's in their terms of service. Oh, I'm sure they got a phone call from somebody that pushes them around. Oh, I mean, you know, you guys, yeah, I mean, okay, actually, we know, we know, one of the guys triggered, it was like a billion dollar fine or something with Google, because of the way it was going to happen. Yeah, you might have known about that. Yeah, I can't, yeah, that's what I was going to start, but I don't want to get too far in a tangent, but I did want to show you guys this Google trend. So, here's the trend of probiotics, and you can see how it's upticking, you know, here's 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010, well, and here is the day we turned on the video, boom. And here is the day they kicked us off, boom, boom. Can anyone guess what this is? Some Dr. Osho. If anyone at home voted, it was legislation. Actually, that trend right there that you were looking at right there was my next probiotic sale letter. Kicking it back up again, and I like to think that all these probiotics companies are now popping up selling probiotic chocolates and probiotic drinks and all that shit, you know, or just like kind of riding this wave. You can see it stayed up a little bit after we got kicked off this wave, you know. But yeah, crazy shit, but you know, I learned a lot about marketing while I was writing this letter. It forced me to dig deep and do a lot of studying, you know, it took me a long time to gather all this information and research it, you know, and it was freaky shit. I learned a lot about health, though, and I take probiotics every single day. I take way more than most doctors recommend. I've noticed improvements in my own health, so you know, I highly recommend it to everybody. But you know, I think a lot of positive came out of this, and I want to also show you guys something that, you know, can, I think, you know, motivate people to create things of impact around this, and actually kind of a sad thing based on the fact that Google is the biggest advertising network and they kind of don't really allow this stuff anymore. But check this out right here. Okay, so USA Today, April 24, 2015, Diapepsy to Ditch the Aspartame. So it's a little over a year of the date when we started this campaign. Diapepsy decided to switch out Aspartame for a more natural sweetener. So, you know, things, you know, who knows if this is the reason why. I mean, you know, this thing was running in a pretty high volume. People were retweeting this thing, too. And here's another side benefit to writing something that has that you can't make this shit up story and is so compelling. This went viral without us ever asking for people to forward it to their friends. So when it was launched, I would do a search on Twitter every day for the hashtag keybiotics, and I would have my cell phone. I'd have the Twitter app open, and I would sit there and go like this and just scroll through the dozens and dozens of people who were just tweeting this thing every day and talking about, well, this crazy story of sugar and I was freaking people out. And then one day, Joe Rogan, who has a stake in a competitive supplement company, tweeted the thing and he's like, you guys got everyone got to see this story about sugar. Joe Rogan's got millions of followers. There was many doctors who were tweeting this thing and telling them, you know, like this thing tells us the true story. So if you're a patient of mine, this is why I've been telling you to avoid sugar. Watch this. So, you know, not only did this create a huge business for, or create, you know, sparked our probiotic business, it got a lot of people taking probiotics who had never thought of it before. And if you go and do, you know, give probiotics themselves a tab test while you're on this webinar, go pop open some tabs and look for what doctors can say about probiotics and what people are saying to take them. I mean, they really do make you feel a lot better. And, you know, I feel really good knowing that, you know, even people don't take keybiotics for the rest of their life, but I know we've turned a lot of people on and created this market for probiotics. And, you know, hopefully we had a little something to do with Aspartame getting boobed out of Pepsi, because as you can see in here, Aspartame is bad shit, and that criminal investigation was 100% justified and real. It's in all the records of Congress. Great. I wanted to ask you that when you're writing a sales, when you're testing something like that, do you do any testing? Do you test the headlines or the colors, or you just go live, or is there a process that you go through before you open the doors to? Yeah, absolutely. This one, though, just hit it out the box as far as the letter content. We made a lot of testing on order pages. We had a page in between the video and the order where people could fill out some information, and it would suggest which probiotic strains would help them with what they were experiencing, which increased conversions. We tested a lot of different price points and packages, background layouts, banner testing, and banners are huge, too. If you're running banners, that could be very important. If you're just doing affiliate traffic, banners aren't quite as big an issue, but if you're getting to six figures a day, one of my other rules I should have put on here is you must have a really compelling banner, because a good banner can double your conversion rate, because the same people did twice as many click on it. That's good for your bottom line. Another thing that happened with this is this was running so huge. Google is a thing where people can hit, like, I don't want to see this and complain. We were just running this so big that people were seeing it everywhere, not to mention retargeting, and all that, but on such a grand scale that a lot of people were hitting that, I don't want to see this. We had all types of problems keeping banners going, because when you're running at a low volume, a couple complaints are going to hurt, but if it's one complaint for 10,000 people, and you're putting in front of millions of people every single day, those things start to scale up. The same thing starts happening with your customer service and all that. We had to start hiring people really quickly when this thing blew up, and just to manage the order tracking and fulfillment, people who clicked on the wrong package size, they only wanted one and they clicked three, and this and that. You've got to have all that stuff in place, too. You mentioned there, Craig, that with the banners, when you have a client like that, do you just write the copy or do you take charge of everything, the marketing, the banners, the ad spend, or is that just a specific bit that you do? So this was, I am one of the owners of this company. However, if you're going to do this for a client, you have to take the entire path. You've got to start from banner to finish. I would do your absolute due diligence, too, to make sure that company is doing their part of fulfilling the orders and getting whatever product that is, making sure the product is great. For example, if it is a supplement, you should make sure that the third party is testing the supplements, because they might be getting ripped off by their own manufacturer not even knowing it. I would say that if you are a writer, you've got to do the whole thing. You've got to look at the email that someone gets after they order it and make sure that has the right copy in there. You've got to look at the inserts that go in the package when they get it. Someone just launched a course on how to start a supplement company. I have mixed emotions about that. It's getting harder and harder to run supplements on the internet. I'll tell you this, I hope they included how frickin' hard it is to run a supplement company, because I'll tell you another thing that we didn't realize is that probiotics are very temperature sensitive. We get our products third party tested all the time. However, we had a batch worth multiple hundreds of thousands of dollars go bad in our warehouse and fail our tests. Then we were like, is it our fault? Is it the people who manufactured and put the seal them up to the standards? Because they told us that they would last in the warehouse and they didn't. Then we had to get into legal shit with this company and we ended up settling out of court. Business is pretty frickin' crazy. If you're just a freelance copywriter, maybe you would want to take a more hands-off approach and just write the copy and stuff. If you're going to hit a big winner and you're in this for royalties, I would go and bring in a frickin' PI or something and make sure they're doing everything right. Also, I feel like if you're doing a big campaign like this, you're taking on a big responsibility. What if the company is not... I don't want to get on too much of a tangent, but what if they're not honoring refunds and shit like that? If I wrote something like that and I felt that the company wasn't honoring the refunds or was selling a crap product or something like that, it wouldn't make me sleeping too well at night. I would suggest that if you're going to do freelance stuff that you do, do diligence, take that advice as well. Okay, I've got one more question and I'm going to let this panel ask some questions. Your process for writing, in a conversation that I had with Kevin once, he said that Gary, when he just wrote top to bottom, that's kind of how I write. Now, David, for example, he will write and go back and have a look at it. I read that John Carlton likes to write the bullet points first. If you're writing something like that, how do you do it? What's your kind of process that goes through your head? I start with clinical studies. This type of thing, I start looking up, does this actually have a benefit in a lab, test an environment and what are those clinical studies? I read them and then I just read what other people are writing about it. When I say other people, I mean forum posts and bloggers and stuff like that. This one required a shift ton of research. The other thing I like to do, it's a great question and there's a lot of different answers depending on what I'm writing. I typically tell people something I learned from Evan Peggin and that is put yourself in the shoes of your customer and go deep and sit there in your head and actually sit down and shut your freaking eyes and do a meditation for five minutes and pick yourself that you are your ideal customer. Pick out some person whether it's like a 46 year old divorced white male engineer in Stanford Connecticut and actually think about him and then open your AMIs and write page long autobiography that you are him. Hi, my name is John. I'm an engineer and I live with my two children because my wife cheated on me two years ago but I'm doing the best and I love my kids and my job is it pays the bills but I'm not super happy with it but I really love golfing on the weekends with the guys and I'm enjoying staying in shape at the gym and this and that. Just write down all the little things that you can think of about them. Then put that aside and then sit down and write your sales letter with the way John Carlton suggests where you're sitting at a bar and you hear this dude John talking at the bartender and explaining the problem to the bartender that you have the exact solution for and then you turn to him and be like, hey man I couldn't help but overhear and you're speaking to the bartender. Here's what I suggest and then write as though you're speaking to that person sitting next to you at the bar. I got that from Carlton many years ago. I think I heard him say that when I had a Dan Kennedy seminar I got up to the microphone and asked him that same damn question. So hopefully that helps. That's great. Thank you Craig. Christine we can't hear you. I've got a question from our, oh not again. We can hear you now. How about now? Okay there we go. I've got a question from one of our members, Joshua, wants to know, if there are any steps that you Craig used when you are thinking about researching a new trend or future prospecting for other products. Is there any particular? Yeah go on Google trends and see what's happening. That's a good one and then just look at the media and what's going on. I'll give you another example. I also own a skincare company called City Cosmetics and City Cosmetics had an offer that was a lip-pumping product that ran very strong for a number of years and then it slowly faded out as all internet offers seemed to do unfortunately. But recently Kylie Jenner was in the media as someone who went and got lip injections and it was like all over Instagram and this and that. And I was like okay maybe we could freshen this up by taking this current news event and talking about like, hey you heard about Kylie Jenner's lip injections. How would you like to have the same results with out needles without pain and for about one-tenth the price if you're interested, pay close attention here. We lost you Kristy. Great thank you. I hate this. How about now? Are we doing now? Yep. Okay I need a new headset in the worst way apparently. So that was a great answer. Thank you so much. I'm curious what the banner ad actually said. Yeah, so the banner ad that did the best for this was a diagram of the human body and it showed it pointed out possible symptoms. So you know typical things that happen to someone when they do have yeast overgrowth would be like indigestion, constipation, fatigue, brain fog, you know less of a concentration inability to get anything done without caffeine, things like that. So it just listed out some of the more usual ones and that was getting good cookers. Yeah, another thing we found about banners is like you know a lot of people are talking about this these days so this is probably not news to anyone but like the weirder the better you know. So like there's a banner right now running for an insurance company it's got a picture of a hand with a fingernail is like growing and not growing and it's for like insurance and it doesn't even do with insurance but people just like hell is this. Then maybe they see the fingers first and then catches their attention and they click on it because the headline is like oh yeah I do need insurance but you know yeah something that stands out. But then you're also playing the banner game it's increasingly tough because the webmaster doesn't want your weir banners on his site and he's doing Google content network or whatever you know. If you're a webmaster if you think about it like who's your dream advertiser well it's usually someone like BMW because it's got like a hot car or like a couple models standing next to it or something like that. It's a luxury brand and it's like you know just makes your site look upscale and you know it's also brand advertising so they're probably giving you a bunch of money and they don't give a shit about anyone. So that's like your dream advertiser you know and then you got these other you know direct response people that are putting up these weird banners and that takes away from your site. Oh and the BMW banner has a bonus and no one ever clicks on it either because they don't like you know so it's got that bonus this one people actually takes them away from your website it looks a little weird on there. You know you don't know exactly what they're selling all that you know so you get the webmasters will be complaining and they won't want to run it if it's too strange or too off putting. And then the other thing is like BMW look at the site and they'll see a weird banner and they're like I don't want this running next to my nice BMW so if you keep accepting advertising for these guys I'm pulling the beamer ads. And that's a you know maybe your banner gets shut down real quick so the banner game is a tough one it's actually could be a topic for a whole nother webinar but I play and win the banner game it's gotten tougher and tougher. That's an interesting thing is do you deal with so it sounds like BMW doesn't care because they do the PPI instead of the PPC is that. Is that why they you know the game is a little better for them because they're not are they paying for the impression are they paying for the clicker. Well we we pay for impression also. Okay. But you got to be able to track you know about how many people are going to click so you know what you can spend on impression you know to figure that. So the only way you can get a big winner these days is paper or you know a huge thing is paper impression there's plenty of big winners though on click bank and things like that that they don't play the banner game they've got affiliates you know like I mean I know. I don't want to reveal the person's business here but I know it's got a hundred and twenty million dollar year operation on the Internet and it's like 80% affiliate traffic isn't due to ban or game he just like has a really strong offers that are very targeted to people's lists he pays out great commissions he sells an excellent product that people buy month after month after month you know so he just plays the affiliate game as the merchant and crushes it you know so there's other ways of making big offers besides banners also. But there's no way to go scale faster than a banner because if you have something that works you can just hit you know on and it's all over the entire Internet and you know you can you can do multiple six figures in a single day if you have a banner plus offer a thing with that. I'm making over so I make this the last question and let me make sure I absorb the whole thing and like I try to hit a halberd style home run on this last one. So. So your question was markers what my advice would be to get a new copyright or first starting out. Yep. Okay. I'm trying to think of something that hasn't been done before because I do tell people all the same thing that Gary said is like you know find the big winners and hand copy them which is critical. I would say immersion. I'm starting a business development. Brand blog or whatever and like what it's going to be totally different copywriting but one of the big things when we talk about their immersion. So the reason I feel I was able to become a skilled copywriter rather quickly is because I was totally immersed in it. So I was I didn't listen to music for three years. I only listened to marketing programs that was driving around in my car and I was writing 10 pages a day of copy and I had to write 10 pages. I had a I was fortunate that I was able to do it for a living when I was working for Evan and he said hey if you write 10 pages of hard hitting copy every single day. I will pay you six figures a year and I was like yeah okay so I'm gonna do this. I was writing 10 pages a single day when I was done writing copy I was reading copy and I woke up every morning I read a Gary Halbert letter. That's what I made myself do every single morning when I was on my lunch break I had the Ogilvy and advertising book for the Cables book and I'm reading that thing during lunch that I'm going back and I'm writing my other five pages. I got addicted to caffeine which I don't recommend. But you know I was just I was just totally immersed I was doing all the marketing programs you know. I cut out a lot of I mean I still have a social life but like you know like just the like fun aspects of hanging around the house like I quit watching TV you know because why would I watch TV I could watch a marketing program. And I was I would say I was just massively immersed for a period about about three years and then you know after that I was able to stay a little bit and you know I was doing it at a less immersive level. I mean something happened to me that was kind of funny and it happens to every copywriter at some time but I've been in the game probably four or five years. I had written some pretty successful stuff that I had this letter that I thought was going to be my best letter ever I like every claim was so proven out. You know the hook was so magical and I thought this thing was going to be the be all and all and it bombed so frickin hard. I was devastated because I spent like I mean I also spent longer writing this thing than I had any other thing I spent like you know two and a half months laboring over this thing. And I just like felt like all that work was wasted and I was like fuck okay I'm going back to school what am I doing wrong. So I pulled out the events of Vanga video that pulled back off the Gary Halbert newsletters you know I had actually got from this evidence about Gary Halbert swipe files I started reading the winning ad. Listen to that you know I just like kind of put myself back in that immersion sort of listening to these programs again and just you know full time analyzing other winners. I started doing the handwriting myself. I remember I hand wrote out truth about abs was a big letter at the time Mike Geary this program I remember I had wrote that out myself you know I started doing that I just put myself back in this immersion school and I came out. Writing better than ever before bigger winners. Now I've been doing this you know a little over 10 years and I never stopped learning like you know I wrote the promotion I share with you guys today in 2013. And you know I would not have been able to write that if it weren't for that second bout of immersion and going and like my first immersion the Internet there was no video sales that is the letters I've hand copying were like Gary's old. Stuff and you know great stuff that have the lessons in there but you know things have changed a little bit like got first of the tab test is something no one thinks about that you're going to get Google to shit like people going to search all your stuff they're going to make a final reclaim them are you know real or not and if your products. Good or not and if it reviews out you know so like. Now you gotta make I mean copywriting like wall it's still I think a fantastic way to make a living and if you're great like you can definitely write your own paychecks but it has gotten a bit harder so you know I haven't had that second immersion into like let's like copy 5.0 or whatever it is then since you know sales advertising. Cloud Hopkins came along you know I found out about how to do video sales letters and beat the tab test and all that shit you know I wouldn't be able to write the promotions that. You know I'm saying well I forgot to focus on this or do that. I was trying to do too many things at once with some of the letters. It was like the letter I wrote it was like a confusing it was like was it a weight loss offer was it a make I get in shape like quick muscles thing you know like was I talking to. Someone who was overweight or was I talking to a guy who wanted to you know get in shape to meet girls or was I talking to a guy who wanted to get in shape to you know fitness pants like you know didn't really know who was talking to it was like very all over the place so I would say and that's what I.
